A kid obsessed with Star Wars, he gave up learning Mandarin soon after starting school. “I just decided I needed to speak English and I spoke only English at home as well.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"In his new show, he talks of the 100 toy lambs that formed the backbone of his childhood between the ages of four and 12. “I was obsessed with them. I kept writing sci-fi stories about them. I wasn’t very social, but I did have all these weird hobbies. So that’s a big part of the show – how I didn’t develop very normally as a kid.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Toy pets aside, Cheng was aware from early on that he would never entirely fit in. “I guess as a kid you also think you’re different because you’re you,” he says, “so it’s very hard to divorce the two.” Cheng recalls the school bullies who would “use racial slurs or say, ‘Jackie Chan!’ and stuff like that. Personal insults have always offended me, not just racial ones. But there are times when you’re like, all right, someone’s reminded me I’m different.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Thoughts of professional comedy would not come until much later, but, Cheng adds, “I remember always playing up that I could mention I’m Chinese and that would be funny to people. I would say something as basic as, ‘Hi, I’m Ken. I’m Chinese,’ and people would just find that funny.”"}]},{"type":"div","attribs":{"class":"methode-html-wrapper"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"\n"},{"subtype":"youtube","type":"iframe","attribs":{"width":"560","height":"315","src":"https://www.youtube.com/embed/uoD-hmW4YUE","frameborder":"0","allow":"autoplay; encrypted-media","allowfullscreen":""},"children":[]}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"One of Cheng’s most popular set pieces revolves around him deconstructing his most hated English proverbs. On “To kill two birds with one stone”: “If you’re going around killing birds with stones, you have issues. If you’re going around killing birds so often that you need a time-saving way of doing it, that is an obsession.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Cheng plans to turn his focus to Chinese phrases next “because I’m kind of bored with all the English ones”. “I like the proverb ‘Don’t add legs to a snake’. It’s essentially saying that more is not necessarily better.” He also cites: “One drop of semen is worth 10 drops of blood,” which, according to "},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"The Routledge Encyclopedia of the Chinese Language"}]},{"type":"text","data":", is a warning of the dangers of Mark Liu’s specialist subject."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Cheng is also active on Twitter. His “Thread where I dis­respect every flag of every country one by one” went viral. He said of China’s flag that its “subliminal message to give it 5 stars earns it 0 STARS”, while Vietnam received the verdict: “If I wanted to see this crap, I would’ve just zoomed in on the Chinese flag.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Hong Kong didn’t make the cut but, when I ask what he makes of the Bauhinia, he comes over all coy. After getting up an image on his phone, he declares: “It’s all right, actually. I think it’s kind of cool. There’s a natural beauty about it.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"But I thought you were meant to slag it off, I protest."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“If I just do Hong Kong in this interview, it will look like I’m really going after Hong Kong,” he says."}]},{"type":"div","attribs":{"class":"methode-html-wrapper"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"\n"},{"type":"blockquote","attribs":{"class":"twitter-tweet","data-lang":"en"},"children":[{"type":"p","attribs":{"lang":"en","dir":"ltr","xml:lang":"en"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"Thread where I disrespect every flag of every country one by one"},{"type":"br","children":[]},{"type":"text","data":"\n"},{"type":"br","children":[]},{"type":"text","data":"\nThe US flag: god it's shite"},{"type":"br","children":[]},{"type":"text","data":"\n"},{"type":"br","children":[]},{"type":"text","data":"\n[1/195] "},{"type":"a","attribs":{"href":"https://t.co/au3hQaHBUg"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"pic.twitter.com/au3hQaHBUg"}]}]},{"type":"text","data":"\n"},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"— Ken Cheng (@kenchengcomedy) "},{"subtype":"twitter","twitter_id":"912267740228018176","type":"a","attribs":{"href":"https://twitter.com/kenchengcomedy/status/912267740228018176?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"September 25, 2017"}]}]}]},{"type":"text","data":"\n"},{"type":"script","attribs":{"async":"","src":"https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js","charset":"utf-8","type":"text/javascript"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"\n\u003c!--//--\u003e\u003c![CDATA[// \u003e\u003c!--\n\n\n//--\u003e\u003c!]]\u003e\n"}]}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Cheng has been less circumspect in the past. In a tweet responding to a BBC article asking if a Gap advert featuring only white children was racist, the comedian proclaimed, “This racist exclusion from adverts must stop. You can’t omit Chinese kids just because they are all ugly as f**k.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“You’ll remind me of tweets that I definitely post but just forget,” he says, explaining that he had already deleted all online communication before 2014 “because I just think, ‘Why risk it?’ I can’t believe politicians are still not doing that."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“Right now, all my stuff is offending racists and I’m not bothered.” His tweet “Terrorism is one of the only areas where white people do most of the work and get none of the credit” has been liked 200,000 times. “But you do run the risk of going too far,” he warns, pointing to comedians who have shown how online outbursts “can boil up into a critical mass of hate”."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Cheng had given no thought to a career in comedy when he began his mathematics degree at Cambridge University. Nor was it where he turned when he dropped out after a year."}]},{"type":"track-viewed-percentage","attribs":{"percentage":"50"}},{"type":"blockquote-quote","attribs":{"class":"blockquote"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"It’s a weird one because there’s a point in poker where you can’t assign that to real things or you’d spend all your time going, ‘Oh, I lost a car, I lost a house’. You have to dissociate yourself from it a bit"},{"type":"text","data":"Ken Cheng"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Not only was he struggling to keep up with the course, his studies were getting in the way of his poker. He had started playing online at school, making a few thousand US dollars here and there. His decision to abandon his degree was the source of several arguments with his disappointed mother, but, he says, he did not feel a sense of failure."}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“I was quite … what’s the word, I was quite triumphant that I actually made a decision about my own life. I was going, ‘I’m making my own path now.’ I’d never done that before.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Gambling would provide Cheng’s sole source of income for more than a decade; he gave it up only this year, to focus on joke-writing."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"He smiles hesitantly when I ask what kind of figures we are talking about. “I hate answering that question because there was one point where I had much more money than I have now.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"He eventually admits that he could make – and lose – more than US$100,000 in a day. “It’s a weird one because there’s a point in poker where you can’t assign that to real things or you’d spend all your time going, ‘Oh, I lost a car, I lost a house’. You have to dissociate yourself from it a bit."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“Obviously, now, when there is more pressure on my finances, what with London rent and me taking a lot of time off to do comedy, which for a while wasn’t making me anything but was just an investment for the future, there’s certainly more pressure.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"img","attribs":{"title":"Cheng on stage at London’s Comedy Store. Picture: Courtesy of Ken Cheng","width":"660","height":"385","data-resolution":"2","src":"https://cdn1.i-scmp.com/sites/default/files/images/methode/2018/06/01/69f73e6a-6245-11e8-82ea-2acc56ad2bf7_1320x770_150017.JPG","class":"caption"},"children":[]}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Furthermore, the adrenaline rush from a successful gig simply cannot compare to a bumper poker win."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“There’s no bigger high than winning US$100,000. You’ve stayed up to 8am and you can’t sleep, the high is so much. And obviously that feeling of financial security is unrivalled. If you win US$100,000 you feel, ‘I will probably never have money problems in my life.’”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Mercifully, the troughs do not match up either."}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“There’s no bigger low than losing money,” Cheng says. “Even a tiny bit of money. Comedy has lows but you can think, ‘Fifty people saw me not be funny for 10 minutes. What’s the damage?’ I felt embarrassed for 10 minutes. But really my life has not been negatively affected.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"a","attribs":{"href":"http://www.scmp.com/news/hong-kong/education-community/article/2096548/meet-hong-kong-australian-comedian-making-fun","target":"_blank","title":"www.scmp.com","data-entity-uuid":"23e06cdf-36bf-480c-97c9-10826b20c201","data-entity-type":"node","data-entity-bundle":"article"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"Meet the Hong Kong-Aussie comic with no shortage of material"}]}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Without the financial cushion afforded by his winnings, Cheng may never have risked his career change. The confidence and emotional development he gained were also crucial."}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“It taught me to go out of my comfort zone, for one. I would have never gone, ‘I’m just going to sign up for that gig.’ It’s very much a poker mindset to be like, ‘OK, I’m going to make a thing happen because, why not?’"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“There’s a big emotional element to poker, a huge one. It helped me handle risk and handle loss. You have to be very rational about everything that happens in poker – and the same in comedy. When you have that rationalisation, you’re like, ‘So what if nobody laughs at me.’"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“I recommend everyone try and get good at poker – it makes them better at life in general. Poker taught me more than any of my first 15 years of education, including a year at Cambridge. Poker taught me how to learn.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"The icing on the cake – if any were needed – is that in Britain, poker winnings come tax-free."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“We are probably the only gambling tax haven in the Western world. I never did tax returns. I have to do one this year because of comedy and I don’t actually know how.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"img","attribs":{"title":"Hsiung Shih-I and his wife, Dymia, in the 1930s.","width":"486","height":"712","data-resolution":"2","src":"https://cdn1.i-scmp.com/sites/default/files/images/methode/2018/06/01/38ba4284-6245-11e8-82ea-2acc56ad2bf7_972x_150017.JPG","class":"caption"},"children":[]}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Cheng’s extraordinary biography does not end there. Remarkably, he discovered the extent of his own cultural heritage only at Christmas, while chatting with his mother."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Hsiung, Cheng’s mother’s grandfather, was, accor­ding to his biographer, Dr Diana Yeh, “a completely unknown student who within weeks literally shot to worldwide fame”."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Hsiung had made a name for himself in China as an adept translator of English film subtitles and plays by the likes of George Bernard Shaw ("},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Pygmalion"}]},{"type":"text","data":") and J.M. Barrie ("},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Peter Pan"}]},{"type":"text","data":") into Chinese. The first play of his own that he pitched to the Brits, after travelling to London in the early 1930s, was not received well. What would now be known as a “kitchen sink drama”, it portrayed the political realities and class divides of contemporary China. “The British weren’t interested in that; they were interested in the exotic never-never land of a China of the ancient past,” explains Yeh, a lecturer at City, University of London, and author of "},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"The Happy Hsiungs: Performing China and the Struggle for Modernity"}]},{"type":"text","data":" (2014)."}]},{"type":"blockquote-quote","attribs":{"class":"blockquote"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"Every once in a while, there’s a sudden China fever; where everyone suddenly wants to do stuff about China – and, obviously, we’re kind of going through one now."},{"type":"text","data":"Diana Yeh, biographer and lecturer"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"It was Hsiung’s second script that captured the public imagination. "},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Lady Precious Stream"}]},{"type":"text","data":" was a modern reworking of the ancient Peking opera "},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Wang Baochuan"}]},{"type":"text","data":". “Keeping a touch of the exotic but making it palatable to British audiences” was what helped the production become the first by a Chinese writer and director to be staged in the West End, and then on Broadway, Yeh says."}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“This is a phenomenal story because there was no Chinese writer having such an impact,” she adds. “There was no other Chinese writer who was known at all. He and the play became household names. He and his wife, Dymia, were interviewed by publications like "},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Good Housekeeping"}]},{"type":"text","data":".”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Queen Mary went to see it in London, along with vast swathes of the diplomatic corps, keen to educate themselves on Chinese customs, while American first lady Eleanor Roosevelt got a ticket for a Broadway performance."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“It received huge critical acclaim but was also very, very politically important,” Yeh says, “and it was also astonishingly popular.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"She adds that “every once in a while, there’s a sudden China fever; where everyone suddenly wants to do stuff about China – and, obviously, we’re kind of going through one now.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"img","attribs":{"title":"The Hsiungs with Chinese-American painter and writer Mai-mai Sze (far left), US first lady Eleanor Roosevelt (second from right) and American theatrical producer Morris Gest (far right), in New York, in 1936.","width":"660","height":"385","data-resolution":"2","src":"https://cdn4.i-scmp.com/sites/default/files/images/methode/2018/06/01/48a2c612-6245-11e8-82ea-2acc56ad2bf7_1320x770_150017.JPG","class":"caption"},"children":[]}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Cheng is perhaps a beneficiary of the latest wave of Sinophilia. He is at the vanguard of a small but mighty group of Asian comedians cracking Britain’s comedy circuit. Others are Phil Wang, Rick Kiesewetter, Yuriko Kotani and Nigel Ng."}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Indeed, it was seeing Chinese-British comedian Wang on stage that put ideas into Cheng’s head."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“The first live gig I saw, Phil Wang was on the bill and that definitely, subconsciously affected me, being like, ‘Well, that’s someone like me up there who can do it,’” says Cheng. “Seeing that definitely makes you think, ‘Well, why don’t I give it a go?’”"}]},{"type":"track-viewed-percentage","attribs":{"percentage":"90"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"On why now seems to be a particular moment for British-born Asian comedians, Wang says, “I think the no-nonsense aspect of the Chinese personality lends itself well to comedy. It provides a funny means by which to dissect the ridiculous aspects of life and society.”"}]},{"type":"blockquote-quote","attribs":{"class":"blockquote"},"children":[{"type":"text","data":"We have a problem with diversity in this country. As in America, there are issues with those who are construed as foreign, as not being part of the British nation’s history"},{"type":"text","data":"Ken Cheng"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"It will be fascinating to see if Cheng goes one further than his forebears. His great-grandparents “did find it difficult to break out of being that exotic ‘other’”, Yeh says, “so they were never fully accepted as modern subjects. They were always seen as, ‘Ooh, the Chinese person’. Rather than a writer, it would be ‘a Chinese writer’."}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“We have a problem with diversity in this country. As in America, there are issues with those who are construed as foreign, as not being part of the British nation’s history.”"}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Cheng is clearly already on the path to becoming much more than “Ken Cheng: Chinese Comedian”."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"“Ken is pure logic distilled into a very funny man with a disarming deadpan delivery,” says Wang. “I think his future is very bright.”"}]},{"type":"inline-ad-slot","attribs":{"tag":"6"}},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Perhaps the mathematician-turned-poker star will go all in to make the leap his great-grandfather never managed. I for one won’t be betting against it."}]},{"type":"p","children":[{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":"Ken Cheng’s new show,"}]},{"type":"text","data":" Best Dad Ever"},{"type":"em","children":[{"type":"text","data":", will run at the Bedlam Theatre as part of the Edinburgh Festival Fringe in metadata.description: More than 85 years after his playwright great-grandfather became a household name in Britain, Ken Cheng is wooing audiences with his own brand of humour … just don’t tell mum and dad
